In parallel with our first half investments, we've also made good progress with our funding strategy, which we outlined at the start of the year and is centered on accretive sales of noncore assets. We recently sold an initial tranche of 15 self-storage operating properties for $175 million executed at a sub-6% cap rate.”

Not only is this a property type upgrade from self-storage to industrial, but selling below 6% and buying above 9% is an excellent spread.

The debt portion of the acquisitions is to be funded with 4.65% senior unsecured notes.

Overall, that represents a nearly 400 basis point spread making the purchases atypically accretive.

Broadstone Net Lease has a massive build-to-suit pipeline of industrial and retail properties tabulated below.

Note that BNL has a market cap of $3.15B, so these near-term developments are about 18% of market cap.

The weighted average cap rate is 7.4% cash and 8.8% GAAP. Again, the difference is the 2.7% escalators.

New properties tend to sell at lower cap rates than older properties because they are just going to be higher quality and require less maintenance. So having the opportunity to develop brand new properties at such a high cap rate is very accretive to BNL’s long-term growth. This pipeline, in combination with BNL’s substantial leverage adjusted discount to the sector, makes it a great opportunity.

Gladstone Commercial had the aforementioned $79 million acquisition pipeline in 2Q25 alone which is a tremendous pace for a roughly $600 million market cap company.

GOOD has an enhanced ability to source high cap rate acquisitions because of their in-house credit underwriting capabilities. Many other buyers of triple net properties are hamstrung into working with large tenants that have gone through the process of getting credit rated by one of the major agencies. There are relatively fewer buyers competing for assets of unrated tenants.

Some of these unrated tenants have excellent credit that simply has not been through the process of getting rated. So by being able to underwrite the credit internally, GOOD can secure sale-leaseback acquisitions with high credit tenants, but get it at the price (meaning higher cap rate) of unrated tenants.

The other benefit of GOOD right now is that the stock is trading quite cheaply which has pushed it all the way to a 9% dividend yield. In combination with the growth from acquisitions, total return potential is double digit.

The bottom line

Triple net REITs have been forsaken in the higher interest rate environment. With other fixed income investments offering reasonably high yield, demand for triple nets has plummeted. However, one thing NNN REITs offer that other fixed income doesn’t is growth. With escalators now getting signed at 3% annually, a significant amount of baseline growth is being locked in for the long term.

Current valuations account for the dividend yields, but the market is not pricing in the growth investors get on top of the yields. I think multiples will re-rate higher as these companies consistently show growth going forward.

data.title: Block: Another Crypto Disappointment
data.description:
Heading into the Q2 2025 earnings report, Block, Inc. (NYSE:XYZ) shareholders wanted to hear how the fintech was expanding into the growing crypto world. Instead, bitcoin trading is still a negative and volatile distraction to the quarterly financials. My investment thesis remains Neutral on the stock due to the valuation equation and the lack of a big crypto catalyst.

Another Miss

Block reported a huge disappointment for Q2, with revenues missing analyst estimates by a wide $240 million, leading to an EPS miss as follows:

As highlighted before, Block is generally valued based on gross profit due to the issue with volatile bitcoin trading revenues. In this case, bitcoin trading revenue in Q2 2025 was down $468 million YoY to only $2.14 billion.

As usual, the prime issue with bitcoin revenues is the lack of profits from the trading. Block only produced an $81 million gross profit from the large level of trading.

Even worse, Block outlined the growth path for Cash App as follows:

  • A scaled P2P network that connects customers to their local communities
  • A broad set of commerce tools that help customers manage their spending
  • Deep banking functionality that supports customers saving, investing, borrowing, and more
  • Simple tools that make it easy for customers to transact in bitcoin

The management team only outlined a crypto priority around designing simple tools for customers completing transactions in bitcoin. The company doesn't mention any plans to expand beyond bitcoin, whether with payments, trading, investing, or even Treasury plans via stablecoins.

On the Q2 2025 earnings call, Block didn't mention the term "crypto" at any point. The fintech recently issued a press release to officially release the bitcoin mining hardware called Proto.

While the company has developed a lot of technology around bitcoin payments, CEO Jack Dorsey hasn't seemed very interested in actually profiting off bitcoin-related trading and payments. As highlighted in prior research, Coinbase (COIN) alone has regularly traded with a market cap double the valuation of Block, while the company appears to again be missing out on the opportunity to lead the industry in the move into stablecoins after the U.S. government approved the Genius Act to usher in the crypto revolution.

Gross Profit Push

Despite the crypto disappointment, Block reported solid metrics for the business. Square grew gross profits by 11% YoY to $1.02 billion, and Cash App boosted gross profits by 16% YoY to $1.5 billion.

The company guided further gross profit gains in Q3 to reach $2.6 billion. Block is definitely seeing momentum in the Square and Cash App units after growth stalled in the last year.

As noted above, Block reported total Q2 revenues of $6.1 billion, but this metric just isn't usable. On top of the bitcoin costs of $2.06 billion, the fintech had transaction-based costs of $1.07 billion, adding up to $3.13 billion in transaction-related costs, counting for nearly 50% of all revenues.

The stock has a market cap of $45 billion, and the guidance now is for a 2025 gross profit of $10.2 billion. The company has solid 14% growth in this key metric, while adjusted operating income is hitting $2 billion with a 20% margin.

Block trades at 28x the forward EPS targets while the valuation dips to just over 20x EPS estimates for 2026. The big key here is that the consensus estimates forecast a nearly 39% boost to EPS in 2026 to reach $3.63 per share, while revenues are only forecast to grow 11%, and the company just missed Q2 EPS targets by a wide $0.06.

The fintech not pushing more into the massive crypto opportunities is very disappointing. Especially considering CEO Jack Dorsey is speculated as Bitcoin’s pseudonymous creator, Satoshi Nakamoto, yet Block hasn't really innovated in this area in a way to benefit the company.

Back on the Q2 2025 earnings call, Mr. Dorsey seemed to suggest a business plan around driving Bitcoin as a currency, not really driving the business into building a crypto platform far beyond just bitcoin:

And right now, stablecoins are predominantly used in a remittance use case. If they do become more payments oriented, we'll be there. I don't think right now that it makes sense for us to offer our own because I just don't think there's a huge differentiator above and beyond what exists and in my view, above and beyond what Bitcoin provides.

Bitcoin still stands alone in it being entirely net new, and it has properties that none of these stablecoins have, which make it a better form of currency than the rest of them. It's certainly proven the case over 16 years as a store value. But our intention is to make sure that Bitcoin becomes the native currency of the Internet and that's used as everyday money. And we think it can get there very quickly.

The stock generally fully prices in the growth opportunities ahead without a crypto driver. Since Block is only pushing towards bitcoin as a payment tool to replace existing payment options, the driver of the business is the growth in Square and Cash App payment volumes, not really any new platform expansion of the TAM.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Block is fully valued for the growth targets of the maturing business. Investors should expect market-like returns over the short term.

data.title: The Strategy Shift In The Markets As Tech Stock Rally Takes A Pause
data.description:
After hitting consecutive record highs, markets appear to be taking a breather as the once red-hot tech sector shows signs of cooling. Jeff Evans, VP, Director and Lead of Empirical Research and PM Support with TD Asset Management discusses what conditions could fuel a further market rally.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Markets are still near record highs despite a few down sessions recently. But are the conditions right for the rally to resume and continue? Joining us now to discuss is Jeff Evans, VP, director, and lead of empirical research in PM support with TD Asset Management.

Jeff, great to have you back on the program.

Jeff Evans: Great to be here. Thanks for having me on.

Greg Bonnell: It's August. Volumes get a little thin. The trade can drift here or there. We have hit numerous new all-time highs this year. Looking forward, what can go right, what can go wrong?

Jeff Evans: So it's a great question. Every time markets are at career highs, and then even more importantly when you have valuations that are also starting to push into to more elevated levels, certainly top of mind for a lot of people what happens next, where do we go from here.

We did a little study in my team a couple of weeks ago, just to put some numbers around this, try to get a base case of what normally happens. We found some data on the S&P back to the 1930s, all the way up until today, and did a really simple study, where we went through and said every week, if the S&P has hit a new all-time high, let's see what happens a year later. Is the market likely to be up, down? And get a rough sense of the probabilities.

It works out to about 70% of the time the market is up a year later. Of course, 30% of the time, market would be flat or down. But those are still pretty good odds. And if the base case is 70% probability of going higher, it usually means that investors should stay invested, ride out any short-term volatility. Over the longer term, the market tends to grind higher.

Now, that said, there are a couple of things that are a little bit different this time that will change those probabilities a little bit. Probably the most important is market concentration. What we've been seeing over the last couple of years is the large 10 stocks, largely the tech stocks, accounting for a greater and greater part of the S&P 500. That's up to almost 40% today. In fact, might even be a little bit more than that last time I checked.

And so, you're in a situation where if something goes wrong with one of those companies-- if they have one little operational blip, a misstep, a miscommunication-- they have a disproportionate impact on the index in a way they didn't have five or 10 years ago. You have to go back to the 1930s to find a period where the market was as concentrated as it is today.

And what that means is, unfortunately, there's always a possibility that a company can have a negative quarter or something goes wrong. And so, if we're thinking about the base case, maybe it's more like a 60% chance the mark gets up a year from now, just because we've got to factor in some-- be a little bit conservative and give some likelihood that something could go wrong with one of those larger companies.

The other side of this is market conditions are getting a little frothy in certain areas. Earnings growth expectations based on consensus numbers are 12% per year of growth every year for the next three years. That's double the long-term average. So we're seeing a lot of optimism from sell-side analysts.

And then we're also, of course, seeing valuations starting to get back to where we saw in the early 2000-- depending on how you measure it, 22 to 24 times on the S&P 500. And again, it just comes back to this argument that if anything goes wrong, if we see any little blips, the market tends to sell those companies down pretty quickly. And if it's one of the big tech stocks, you can have a disproportionate impact on the index.

So our base case is, things are still going to evolve reasonably fairly. We're seeing positive earnings revisions across the market. If you get a couple central bank rate cuts as well, that certainly doesn't hurt. But I think we would just be a little bit more cautious given all of the issues above.

Greg Bonnell: When we think about those momentum players that have been leading the market-- it's only been a couple of days, but there was a bit of selling pressure on those. Are we seeing any rotation in the markets?

Jeff Evans: Little bits and pieces. What I would say, interestingly enough, the momentum, if you look at it quantitatively, it's a very slight decline. There have certainly been some bigger tech stocks. But on a broad basis, we're not seeing a huge rotation out of momentum. It's really a couple of AI-specific names.

But what we have seen more of-- and we're noticing this across our quantitative models-- is there's been an uptick in demand for dividend yield and value stocks. Now, what's interesting is that's actually not just the last couple of weeks. It's been going on for five years.

If you go back to November 2020, when we had the vaccine news, bottom of the market, a basket of value stocks has outperformed the S&P 500-- I forget the exact number, but it's 10-15% over the last five years, which probably surprised people, because AI and NVIDIA (NVDA) and all these other companies have been much more front and center. And we're starting to see that trade re-accelerate again, and particularly for dividend yield, now that we're seeing some central bank rate cuts in the US come back on the table. Who knows if it's one or two or four or five. That will depend on how aggressive this rotation is and how long it continues.

But as investors see some of those rate cuts coming in, they've got to replace that income somehow. And that makes dividend yield stocks a lot more attractive. So we've definitely started to see-- maybe not outright selling of the high-multiple stocks, but certainly starting to see investors being aware that they need to diversify away from a concentrated market. And it seems like value and dividend stocks are picking up a lot of that in the couple of weeks.

Greg Bonnell: I hate to say it because it makes people angry at me, but here in Toronto, the CNE is on. Central bankers are gathering in Jackson Hole. We know summer doesn't have that much more life-- at least the proper summer vacation before Labor Day. We all get in serious mode on the other side of Labor Day. Is it still a risk-on market?

Jeff Evans: What we've been seeing in our factory model is, it is very much a risk-on market. And the way we would define that is by looking at high-volatility stocks relative to low. We run that through a lot of our models to get a very clean sense of how the market is pricing those groups.

And what we've been seeing for the last five years-- it is almost the same as value stocks-- the market has had a very strong preference for higher-risk, higher-beta securities-- very unusual. If you look at the year trend, all you have to do is buy boring, defensive, dividend-paying stocks. They tend to outperform with lower volatility.

But the last five years, you're seeing a little more inflation concerns. That tends to help support some of the more cyclical areas-- energy and materials in 2021, 2022. When inflation was heating up, those were quite strong. And then-- people sometimes forget this-- but the NVIDIAs and the AI stocks also tend to be higher-beta relative to the broader benchmark. And so, on this AI theme, we're also seeing that demand holding in.

We really haven't seen-- there's been blips. If you go back to April, when we had the tariff war-- very aggressive rotation into defensive stocks in a short period of time. But broadly speaking, we're right back up to all-time highs in the last five years on our risk-on.

Our main concern would be, this thing seems to be changing very quickly. So if the tone shifts, if something happens, it brings more concern into the market or more macro shocks. We had one of the biggest rotations into defensive stocks in April that we've seen in 25 years, and it happened in the space of a couple of weeks. So it's very important to get ahead of that. We think it's a great time for investors to use the quiet of the summer to add a little diversification and make sure they've got portfolios that are resilient to all market environments.

Original Post

data.title: Moog: The Pain Is Temporary, The Growth Is Long-Term
data.description:
Moog (NYSE:MOG.A) (NYSE:MOG.B), a key Tier 1 supplier to the aerospace and defense industry, has shown underwhelming performance with a 4.3% gain compared to the S&P 500’s gain since my last report in June. While the stock reached my price target, continued tariff concerns and delivery delays on single aisle programs remain a key concern. In this report, I discuss the headwinds the company faces, the most recent earnings, the updated guidance, and establish a new price target for the stock.

Moog Is Slow, Offsetting Tariff Impacts

In my prior report, I already indicated that while many aerospace suppliers saw an impact from tariffs, they were also upbeat about their ability to offset tariff impacts. Moog’s tone, however, was more negative and continues to be cautious. It has given me the impression that, relative to its peers, Moog was simply not fast enough in implementing mitigation efforts.

Patrick J. Roche, CEO of Moog, said the following during the Q2 2025 earnings call on the tariff topic:

Before I finish on the macro environment, I want to share a brief update on tariffs. As a reminder, the most relevant tariffs to our business arise from the import of steel and aluminum and the import of goods from key facilities and suppliers in Costa Rica, the Philippines, Mexico, the European Union, Canada and the United Kingdom. In this quarter, we faced a 25% tariff on steel and aluminum and a 10% country tariff during the so-called 90-day pause. The administration continues to evolve its trade policy and tariff regime as it negotiates with trading partners. We expect that this leads to an increase in country tariffs during the fourth quarter.

We implemented specific actions to mitigate the impact of these tariffs in the quarter. These steps included the use of the U.S.- Mexico-Canada agreement, the effective administration of import and reexport of repair goods, and price adjustments to reflect our new cost base. These mitigations have been effective in reducing tariff impact, but they were not in place for the full quarter due to the timing of implementation. We will continue to mature our approach on tariffs. Operationally, we expect that these actions will limit tariff impact to the range indicated 90 days ago. We are now incorporating that tariff impact into our guidance.

The way I read this is that the company is successfully incorporating mitigations, but their approach is not systematically different from what we already saw peers announcing a quarter ago. The fact that the company announces these mitigation measures now largely tells me they simply watched what peers did before doing it themselves. Moog’s global footprint is not helping their effort to offset impacts.

The second challenge the company faces is the slow pick up in single aisle production rates, and that's a pain felt throughout the industry, while the company had already lowered its expectations for the Airbus A350 program in the prior quarter. Impacts of tariffs and slowness on some commercial programs have been offset through selling non-core assets during the quarter.

Moog Sales And Margins Grow Despite Tariffs

Important to note is that while Moog has seen tariff pressures, its Q3 2025 sales showed 7% growth, bringing the total to $971 million and surpassing analyst estimates by $50 million. Sales in space and defense grew 11%, driven by strength across the portfolio and particularly for missile control systems and satellite components. This aligns with missiles, missile defense, and space being some of the high in-demand products at this moment. Military sales, like in prior quarters, saw expanding sales driven by the FLRAA (Future Long-Range Assault Aircraft) platform. Commercial Aircraft sales grew 16%, driven by strong aftermarket sales, while OEM sales were flat with lower sales on business jet and narrow-body programs. Industrial sales were down 4%, driven by divestitures.

The slide above really speaks for itself. While Moog is experiencing tariff pressures, the sales during the quarter were impressive.

During the third quarter, the margins improved 1.3 points to 13.6%. Space and defense saw a favorable mix with volume driving sales, while military aircraft margins were down due to R&D expenses, part of which is related to the Collaborative Combat Aircraft program, and an unfavorable sales mix. Commercial Aircraft margins increased from 13.1% to 14.9%. The strength there is the usual story of high aftermarket activity partially offset by OEM delays and tariffs. However, where Moog’s commercial margins differ is that its margins in the quarter were boosted by the sale of a non-core product line, which drove margins by 300 basis points. Absent of this sale, the margins in the segment would have been 11.9%. Industrial margins improved, driven by a rationalization of its industrial footprint.

Absent of the divestiture in the commercial airplane business, the margins would have been 12.9%, indicating a 60 basis point expansion in the margins. Earnings per share were $2.37, exceeding analysts’ expectations by $0.22.

Overall, I believe the results were good, and there have also been some positives in the updated guidance. The total sales are now expected to be $3.78 billion, driven by all segments. On margins, there is a 20 basis point reduction after a 50 basis point impact from tariffs. EPS guidance has been lifted by $0.05 while adjusted EBITDA guidance remained stable. Free cash flow conversion is expected to be materially weaker than earlier anticipated, driven by higher investments in working capital.

Moog Stock Is Still A Buy Despite Tariff Headwinds

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

When I started reviewing the comments on tariffs and the downward revision to the guidance, I wasn't impressed and expected that I would be downgrading the stock to "Hold." However, the tariff pressures as well as the single aisle production ramp-up delays are, in my view, transient. Moog is maturing its tariff mitigation efforts, and single aisle production will eventually pick up. That leaves us with a story of improving EBITDA margins and growth in its defense and commercial airplane business lines while the industrial segment footprint has been rationalized. For quite some time, Moog has not really performed well due to cost overruns on a space vehicle program. The result is that while the long-term prospects are bright in key end-markets, the median EV/EBITDA for Moog doesn't reflect that while its leverage in the years ahead is substantially better than that of peers. This could drive the company to increase its share repurchase program.

My price target for Moog is $241.74, indicating 25% upside. For FY 2025 and FY 2026, EBITDA estimates have barely changed while free cash flow generation has come down substantially. The fact that the stock still represents upside is driven by the higher EV/EBITDA median, providing support to my view of expanding EV/EBITDA multiples.

Conclusion: Moog Remains Appealing

I'll be the first to admit that Moog’s earnings and guidance look somewhat uninspiring, but sometimes that's a good thing. We note that the company continues to expand margins despite tariff headwinds and OEM delays. That has led to a lackluster upside for FY 2026, but the FY 2027 upside is looking significantly better and provides support to reiterate my "Buy" rating as OEMs are just at the start of substantially increasing production through the end of this decade, providing Moog with an opportunity to grow sales, margins, and free cash flow.

data.title: HeartFlow: Growth Mirage In A Crowded Market
data.description:
HeartFlow, Inc. (NASDAQ:HTFL) presents an unattractive risk/reward proposition: its large and attractive market opportunity invites heightened competition; the business remains reliant on a single product for virtually all its revenue; it is open to pricing pressures from its key customers; it has a history of fast growth without sustainable value creation; and the share price is justifiable only if margins surge and the company grows at a high rate for an improbably long time. I assign a “Sell” rating to the company. .

Betting Everything on HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis

HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis, the firm’s product for calculating blood flow and determining clinically significant coronary artery disease (CAD), accounts for 99% of the firm’s revenue, with the firm working toward commercialising its second product, HeartFlow Plaque Analysis, which assesses coronary plaque in order to optimise treatment strategies. The firm has vowed to increase its sales of HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis while also raising sales of HeartFlow Plaque Analysis, in order to better diversify its revenue streams.

Customer Relationships Pose Risks Despite Broad Base

Although none of the company’s 1,100 customers contributes 10% or more of revenue, HeartFlow’s reliance on large health systems that operate many accounts creates exposure. The firm’s two largest customers collectively contributed 8% of revenue as of the end of 2024. Any deterioration in these relationships could adversely affect revenue, and this reality reduces the firm’s bargaining power. As management acknowledges:

These customers could also potentially pressure us to reduce the prices we charge for the HeartFlow Platform, which could have a material adverse effect on our margins and business.

This has, I think, implications for the firm’s ability to improve its NOPAT margins sufficiently to justify its present valuation, as the reader will see in the discussion on valuation.

The Perverse Effects of a Large Market Opportunity

According to the S-1/A filing, management believes that the HeartFlow Platform is, “the first and most widely-adopted AI-powered test for CAD”, boasting

a competitively differentiated data set of approximately 110 million annotated images, which is primarily sourced from our commercial relationships with customers, driving training and refinement of our algorithms for over 10 years and the ability to train new AI models for future products.

The size of its market is fairly large, with the company estimating that the market opportunity for HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis and HeartFlow Plaque Analysis is currently worth some $5 billion, with “9.5 million unique stable chest pain patients” receiving non-invasive tests ((NITs)) every year. Management estimates that there are 3.1 million people eligible for its HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis, which, at $1,067, leads to a market opportunity of $3.3 billion. The HeartFlow Plaque Analysis is applicable to 60% of those 9.5 million NIT patients a year, and the bulk of patients experiencing acute chest pain. Management expects that HeartFlow Plaque Analysis has a market opportunity of around 5.5 million patients, which, at a sales price of $300, translates to a $1.7 billion incremental market opportunity.

However, large market opportunities attract capital, and this has the effect of driving down returns on invested capital (ROIC) down toward the cost of capital, largely because the profit available for the taking tends to be overestimated, leading to overinvestment, something which HeartFlow itself acknowledges, saying,

Our estimate of the addressable market for our current products and any future products is subject to significant uncertainty and is based on assumptions and estimates, including our internal analysis and industry experience.

As the investor Peter Thiel once said, “competition is for losers”. The enemy of profitability, competition is significant in an era of “rapid technological change”. Management says that,

Because of the size of the market opportunity for the treatment of CAD, we believe current and potential future competitors will dedicate significant resources to aggressively promote their products or develop new products or treatments. Our principal competition comes from companies that provide traditional non-invasive tests that aid physicians in the evaluation of CAD, such as SPECT, stress echocardiography and PET. Established, traditional non-invasive tests for CAD have been used for many years and are therefore difficult to change or supplement. Many of the companies that sell these traditional non-invasive tests or the equipment they require have established relationships with healthcare providers. One of the major hurdles to adoption of our products is overcoming established testing patterns, which requires education of physicians and supportive clinical data. The companies that sell the traditional non-invasive tests for CAD include companies that offer: (i) cardiac specific tests to primary care and cardiology offices, such as manufacturers of capital equipment for stress echocardiography and SPECT, including GE Healthcare, Siemens Healthineers AG and Koninklijke Philips N.V.; and (ii) products used for the invasive FFR testing market. With the greater resources of some of these competitors and their more diversified product offerings, it is possible that they or other entrants into the market may develop competing products or technologies that could be more effective, accurate, reliable, cost-effective, more advanced or otherwise improved relative to the HeartFlow Platform, which could render our products obsolete or less competitive.

The firm also faces competition from firms using AI-driven platforms that use coronary computed tomography angiography (CCTA) to diagnose CAD, such as Cleerly, Inc., Elucid Bioimaging Inc. and Keya Medical Technology Co., Ltd. Furthermore, computed tomography (CT) scanner manufacturers such as Siemens Healthineers AG (OTCPK:SMMNY), GE HealthCare Technologies Inc. (GEHC), Koninklijke Philips N.V. (PHG), Hitachi, Ltd. (OTCPK:HTHIY), Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd., and Canon Medical Systems Corporation, who are larger, more diversified, and better resourced, could develop the capability to compete with or render incompatible the HeartFlow Platform, which needs a CT image from a CT scan in order to function. The import of this competition is that the probability of any material increase in profitability by the firm is very low, even if it can grow at a high rate.

Category I Codes Make HeartFlow Compelling

The often inflexible reimbursement landscape is critical for diagnostic innovators such as HeartFlow. Whereas pharmaceuticals are typically reimbursed according to value, diagnostic tests are typically reimbursed on a cost basis, governed by fixed rates under procedural coding schemes such as the Current Procedural Terminology (CPT) in the United States. This cost-basis structure often fails to account for the value that emerges from innovation and may even penalise high-impact diagnostics. Manufacturers have even complained that diagnostic related groups (DRG) and tariff systems are not updated frequently enough, so that reimbursement falls short of reflecting clinical utility.

Moreover, relations between regulators, payers and developers remain strained, with one systematic review of personalised medicine reimbursement models calling for early, pre-approval dialogue with payers, along with coverage with evidence development or performance-based agreements, in order to smooth the pathway to adoption. Absent such collaboration and adaptive reimbursement frameworks, innovations such as HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis may face difficulties with access or commercial marginalisation, even if they show clinical merit.

To this, management says that,

We believe current reimbursement policies support the adoption of the HeartFlow Platform. Our HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis is reimbursed under a dedicated Category I Current Procedural Terminology (“CPT”) code, effective as of January 1, 2024, and has established coverage policies representing approximately 99% of covered lives in the United States. A Category I CPT code was recently established for HeartFlow Plaque Analysis. It will go into effect on January 1, 2026, and is covered by all seven Medicare administrative contractor (“MACs”). A Category I CPT code designates a procedure or service that uses device(s) with Food and Drug Administration (“FDA”) clearance or approval (when required), is performed by many physicians across the United States for its intended clinical use, aligns with current medical practice, and has documented efficacy in literature. The Category I CPT status for our HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis and HeartFlow Plaque Analysis validates their widespread use and distinguish them from emerging technologies that are assigned Category III CPT codes.

The implication is that HeartFlow’s services are clinically valid and widely accepted, and, thanks to the Category 1 codes, they enjoy payer acceptance, reducing claim denials, and offering smoother deployment. This is a competitive advantage compared to competitors with Category III codes, who are subject to scrutiny and denial, giving HeartFlow a distinct adoption advantage. With the upcoming Category I code for HeartFlow Plaque Analysis, HeartFlow is attractively positioned in payer systems even before its full commercial launch.

Of course, it should be noted that these factors do not guarantee physician adoption or fend off competition, but they do ensure market-access by reducing payer friction. Furthermore, the investor should not confuse reimbursement stability with pricing power, as Medicare sets fixed fees, and private insurers tend to anchor to them.

Pathway Dependence is an Obstacle

Many investors often imagine that quality innovations will be quickly adopted by the market. However, there is persuasive evidence that even where clinical data is persuasive and payment mechanisms are aligned, there is a tendency toward inertia within healthcare systems that thwarts the adoption of superior innovations. Both physicians and institutions are often loath to adopt new innovations, preferring to maintain established clinical routines, even when there is persuasive data supporting novel approaches. This clinical inertia, especially in the management of hypertension, often occurs as healthcare providers typically defer or ignore alerts to adjust treatment, with one study showing that inertia of practice accounts for 66% of resistance to change. Clinical inertia is important not merely at the physician level but at the systemic level. The diffusion of innovation is not simply the product of having superior technology. HeartFlow must overcome both clinical inertia and the absence of social pressures to prompt adoption.

Growth in the Absence of Value

Between 2023 and the last twelve months (LTM), HeartFlow’s revenue compounded by 24.98% a year, although the company’s one-year revenue CAGR was “only” 8.23%, which is below the 14.8% mean and greater than the 5.8% median global base rates.

Growth has not been accompanied by profitability, with the firm’s net operating profit after tax (NOPAT) rising from -$48.18 million in 2023 to -$20.81 million in the LTM. The increase in the firm’s albeit negative NOPAT was the result of its NOPAT margin growing from -55.27% to -15.29% in that time. In tandem with this, a decline in HeartFlow’s invested capital turns, from 3.36 to 2.2, led to its ROIC rising from -185.65% to -33.66%.

Capital allocation is management’s most important task, and its goal is the creation of value. The dollar value of this is known as economic profit, and tells us how much management earned in excess of what could have been earned on the market. HeartFlow has not created sustainable value, generating -$26.61 million in economic losses in the LTM.

The failure to achieve profitability has meant that HeartFlow has not been able to generate positive free cash flow (FCF), with the company’s FCF growing nonetheless from -$100.08 million in 2023 to -$18.37 million in the LTM, with LTM FCF having a yield of -1.2%.

Only Enormous Profitability Can Justify Its Price

Using my reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model, I unearthed the performance expectations that justify the firm’s valuation at the current price, $30.67 at time of writing, and found that the firm is being priced for a profitability that it has so far failed to deliver.

In order to justify its share price wherein the company has a price-to-economic book value (PEBV) of -9.45, HeartFlow will have to

  • compound revenue at 24.98% annually and,

  • immediately achieve a NOPAT margin of 2.96%.

In so doing, HeartFlow would earn $171.08 billion in revenue, and $5.06 billion in NOPAT by 2056, when the shareholder value per share equals the current share price, and the firm has a market-implied competitive advantage period (MICAP) of 31 years. By way of reference, in the LTM, Siemens Healthineers AG generated €23.38 billion (~$26.99 billion) in revenue and €3.22 billion (~$3.71 billion) in earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT), while GE HealthCare Technologies Inc. generated $19.97 billion in revenue and $3.11 billion in EBIT, and Koninklijke Philips N.V. generated €17.86 billion (~$20.62 billion) in revenue and €1.06 billion ($1.22 billion) in EBIT. For further emphasis, just 2.5% of companies compounded revenue by over 25% for a decade, between 1950 and 2015.

If the firm fails to live up to these expectations, the stock carries significant downsides. For instance, if,

  • revenue compounds by an impressive 20% a year, and,

  • NOPAT margin immediately improves to 5.92%, then,

HeartFlow’s stock is worth $15.94 a share today, a 48.03% downside to the current price, with the firm generating $46.55 billion in revenue and $2.75 billion in NOPAT by 2056. If the firm cannot so rapidly improve its margins or its growth prospects dim, then the potential downsides are even greater.

For instance, if

  • revenue compounds by 16% a year, and,

  • NOPAT margin surges to 11.83%, then,

HeartFlow’s stock is worth $3.26 a share today, a downside of 89.37% to the current price, with the firm generating $11.92 billion in revenue and $705.53 million in NOPAT by 2056.

In these scenarios, HeartFlow grows revenue, NOPAT and FCF, without having to increase its incremental net working capital rate or its incremental total adjusted fixed assets rate. This is highly unlikely though, and any such increases to the rate at which it grows its invested capital would create even greater downsides for investors.

Recommendation

With the significant risks inherent in the business and its current price, I assign a “Sell” rating. While HeartFlow has certainly improved its NOPAT margin in recent years, this has been from a low base, and it is unlikely that it can attain the NOPAT margin levels implied by its valuation, at least within the near-term. This is because pricing pressure, customer bargaining power, and intensifying competition, make it unlikely that the company can achieve and sustain the positive NOPAT margin levels necessary to justify its valuation. Furthermore, the odds of the firm sustaining a high growth rate throughout its MICAP are statistically fairly low. The prospect of even greater competition in an already competitive field, the dependence on a single product, and the vulnerabilities related to customer accounts, narrow the paths to the sustainable growth and profitability implied by the company’s valuation.

Summary of Accounting Adjustments

Here below are details of adjustments made to HeartFlow's S-1/A filing for the LTM:

Income Statement: I made $88.39 million in adjustments to calculate NOPAT, adjustments that are equal to 81.96% of the absolute value of HeartFlow's GAAP net income.

Balance Sheet: I made $158.04 million in adjustments to calculate invested capital with a net increase of $123.82 million. HeartFlow's $102.98 million in excess cash was the largest adjustment, equal to 55.83% of HeartFlow's reported assets.

Valuation: I made $318.58 million in adjustments with a net effect of decreasing shareholder value by $112.52 million, with the fair value of total debt, at $214.4 million, being the largest adjustment to shareholder value, representing nearly 8.34% of HeartFlow’s market cap.
